1:
On Exodus 12:1 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron in the land of Egypt, saying” — the witnesses mark the giving of the Passover ordinance.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the setting. Keil and Delitzsch: a divine consecration was needed, that their outward severance from Egypt be joined to an inward severance from all that was heathen; this the Passover imparted. Ellicott: this section reads as a part of the Law, set down with the institution. The Reformed Calvin: a solemn symbol and memorial of their redemption. Wesley: the Lord had spoken before the darkness, but the mention is put here.

The verse opens the Passover. God gives the ordinance to Moses and Aaron in Egypt itself. The institution is set before the deliverance.

So the witnesses see God, on the eve of the great deliverance, give to Moses and Aaron the ordinance of the Passover. The witnesses note the setting: “in the land of Egypt” — the command is given while Israel is still in bondage, before the firstborn are slain and before they leave; the rite is appointed for the very night of their redemption. They mark Keil and Delitzsch’s observation that a divine consecration was needful, that the people’s outward severance from Egypt might be accompanied by an inward severance from everything heathen; the Passover was to be this consecration, marking them off as God’s redeemed people. They observe Ellicott’s note that this section has the character of a piece of the Law, the institution of the feast set down in order. They observe Calvin’s sense that the Passover was from the first a solemn symbol and memorial of their redemption, whereby the people professed themselves God’s. They note Wesley’s point on the placing of the narrative: God had spoken these things before the three days’ darkness, but Moses records them here, that the account of the plagues might not be interrupted. They observe that the redemption is framed by a sacred rite given beforehand: God ordains how his people shall be saved and how they shall remember it. They note that the whole chapter unfolds from this opening word. The verse thus opens the Passover: God speaks the ordinance in Egypt — beginning, in the next verse, with a new reckoning of the year.
2:
On Exodus 12:2 — “This month shall be unto you the beginning of months: it shall be the first month of the year to you” — the witnesses mark the new reckoning of the year.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the change. Adam Clarke: God now changed the year’s commencement; the month Abib answers to part of March and April. Ellicott: hitherto they began with Tisri, near the autumn equinox; now with Abib in spring. Wesley: they had begun from mid-September, now from mid-March, at least for ecclesiastical reckoning. The Reformed Gill: not only the first but the chief of months, famous for their coming out of Egypt.

The verse appoints a new beginning. The month of the Exodus becomes the first of the year. Redemption resets the calendar.

So the witnesses see God mark the redemption by making its month the head of the year. The witnesses note the institution: “this month shall be unto you the beginning of months” — the month Abib (later Nisan), in which Israel came out of Egypt, is set as the first month, the expositors explaining that it answers to part of our March and April. They mark the change this made, as Ellicott and Wesley observe: hitherto the Hebrews had begun their year in the autumn, near the equinox (with Tisri), as the civil reckoning; now they were to begin it in the spring, at least for their sacred and ecclesiastical calendar, dating the year from the month of their deliverance. They observe Gill’s point that this month was made not only the first but the chief of months, made famous forever by their coming out of Egypt — and, he adds, more famous still in after-times for the sufferings and death of the Messiah, which fell in this same season. They note the significance the witnesses find in the resetting of the calendar: the redemption from Egypt was so great a mercy that it began a new era, time itself henceforth reckoned from it; their national and religious life dated from the act of salvation. They observe that as the deliverance was a new birth for the nation, so it gave a new beginning to their year. They note that God thus stamps the memory of redemption on the very measure of their days. The verse thus appoints the new reckoning: the month of the Exodus the first of the year — the calendar in which the Passover is now to be set.
3:
On Exodus 12:3 — “Speak ye unto all the congregation of Israel… In the tenth day of this month they shall take to them every man a lamb… a lamb for an house” — the witnesses mark the taking of the lamb.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the particulars. Adam Clarke: in this first Passover they took the lamb on the tenth, in later times on the thirteenth. Ellicott: the four days’ interval gave time for careful inspection of the animal. The Reformed Calvin asks why a lamb in every house, when one Lamb reconciles the Church. Wesley: every family, or two small ones joined, a lamb.

The verse begins the rite. On the tenth day each household takes a lamb. The lamb is provided for every family.

So the witnesses see the rite begin with the taking of a lamb for each household. The witnesses note the command: “in the tenth day of this month they shall take to them every man a lamb… a lamb for an house” — on the tenth of Abib, each family is to set apart a lamb. They mark the expositors’ note on the timing, peculiar to this first Passover: the lamb was taken on the tenth and kept till the fourteenth, a four days’ interval; in later observances, Clarke notes, the lamb was taken nearer the day. They observe Ellicott’s point that the four days allowed careful inspection, that the animal might be proved entirely free from blemish. They note the household character of the rite: “a lamb for an house” — each family was to have its own lamb, the feast a domestic as well as a national act. They observe the question Calvin raises and answers: why, when one Lamb alone reconciles the whole Church and God is propitiated by the blood of one Christ, should a lamb be slain in every house? — the answer being that the one sacrifice of Christ must be personally appropriated by each household and soul; the universal redemption is applied to every family. They note that the rite is addressed to “all the congregation,” through the elders, that none be left out. They observe that the provision of a lamb for every house foreshadows the personal application of the one Lamb of God. The verse thus begins the rite: a lamb taken for each household on the tenth — whose sufficiency and division the next verse provides for.
4:
On Exodus 12:4 — “And if the household be too little for the lamb, let him and his neighbour next unto his house take it according to the number of the souls” — the witnesses mark the provision for small households.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: where a family was too small to consume a whole lamb at a sitting, men were to club with their neighbours. Adam Clarke: two families must join; the rabbins required at least ten persons to a lamb, not more than twenty.

The verse provides for the small family. Too few for a lamb, they join with a neighbour. None is excluded for fewness.

So the witnesses see the rite framed so that no household, however small, should be without its share in the lamb. The witnesses note the provision: “if the household be too little for the lamb, let him and his neighbour next unto his house take it” — where a family was not large enough to eat a whole lamb at one sitting, two neighbouring households were to join together over one lamb. They mark the measure of the joining: “according to the number of the souls; every man according to his eating shall make your count” — the reckoning was by the number who would partake, that the lamb might be wholly consumed and none wasted. They observe the rabbinic detail the expositors record, that there should be not fewer than ten persons to a paschal lamb, nor more than twenty — a company sufficient to eat it but not so many as to leave each unsatisfied. They note the care the rite takes for the poor and the few: smallness of family was no bar; the neighbour’s house supplied what the lamb required. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: as the one lamb served a fit company, so the one Christ is sufficient for all who are gathered to him, and none is shut out for being few or poor. They note the communal warmth of the feast, neighbour joined to neighbour about the lamb. They observe that the provision ensures the whole lamb is eaten, as the rite requires. The verse thus provides for the small household: neighbour joined to neighbour over the lamb — the lamb itself, the next verse specifies, to be without blemish.
5:
On Exodus 12:5 — “Your lamb shall be without blemish, a male of the first year: ye shall take it out from the sheep, or from the goats” — the witnesses mark the unblemished lamb, a type of Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the requirements. Keil and Delitzsch: tamim, integer, without bodily fault, as all the sacrifices (Leviticus 22:19); a male, of the first year; from sheep or goats. Ellicott: we must give God our best (Malachi 1:8). The Reformed Gill: a type of Christ, who is said to be without blemish; Maimonides reckoned fifty blemishes. Calvin: the expiation not legitimate unless without fault.

The verse specifies the lamb. It must be without blemish, a male of the first year, of sheep or goats. The spotless lamb prefigures the sinless Christ.

So the witnesses see the chief requirement of the lamb — that it be without blemish — and find in it a plain type of Christ. The witnesses note the qualifications: “without blemish, a male of the first year” — the lamb must be perfect, free of every bodily fault or defect, in the vigour of its first year, and might be taken “from the sheep, or from the goats” (a kid as well as a lamb). They mark the lesson the expositors draw from the demand for perfection: Ellicott notes that natural piety itself teaches we must not offer the blind, lame, or sick, but give God our best (Malachi 1:8); Calvin observes that the expiation was not legitimate unless the victim was wholly without fault, the people thereby reminded of the purity the true sacrifice required. They observe the typology, which several of the witnesses press: the spotless lamb prefigures Christ, the Lamb of God “without blemish and without spot” (1 Peter 1:19), the sinless Saviour offered for sin; Gill expressly calls it a type of Christ who is without blemish. They note that the perfection of the paschal lamb pointed forward to the moral perfection of Christ, who alone could be a fit offering. They observe that the rabbis multiplied the catalogue of disqualifying blemishes, magnifying the demand for an unblemished victim. They note that the lamb’s freedom from fault is the foundation of its fitness to be slain for the people. The verse thus specifies the lamb: spotless, of the first year — the type of the sinless Christ, kept up, as the next verse says, until the fourteenth.
6:
On Exodus 12:6 — “And ye shall keep it up until the fourteenth day… and the whole assembly of the congregation of Israel shall kill it in the evening” — the witnesses mark the keeping and killing of the lamb.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the timing. Keil and Delitzsch and Clarke: the lamb taken on the tenth, kept and fed apart till the fourteenth, then sacrificed; this keeping-up was never commanded afterwards. Ellicott: kept in custody, separate from the flock, for careful inspection. The Reformed Gill draws the type: the preservation of Christ till the appointed time of his sufferings, slain on this very day.

The verse fixes the time. The lamb is kept till the fourteenth, then killed in the evening by all the assembly. The set time and the whole congregation mark the sacrifice.

So the witnesses see the lamb kept apart and then slain by the whole assembly at the appointed hour. The witnesses note the keeping: “ye shall keep it up until the fourteenth day” — the lamb, taken on the tenth, was kept and tended by itself for four days before being sacrificed; the expositors note that this keeping-up for four days was peculiar to the first Passover and never commanded again. They mark the purpose Ellicott gives, that the lamb was held in custody, separate from the flock, that it might be thoroughly inspected and proved without blemish. They observe the time of the killing: “in the evening” — literally “between the two evenings,” the late afternoon of the fourteenth, toward sunset; and the agents: “the whole assembly of the congregation of Israel shall kill it” — each household slaying its own lamb, yet all as one congregation, at the same hour, the act both personal and corporate. They observe the typology Gill and others draw out: the keeping of the lamb till the set day pictures the preservation of Christ until the appointed time of his sufferings; and they note the striking correspondence that Christ was crucified on this very day, the fourteenth, at the very season the paschal lambs were slain. They note that the fixed time and the universal participation mark the solemnity of the sacrifice. They observe that the lamb, proved and kept, is now offered. The verse thus fixes the killing: the lamb slain by all Israel in the evening of the fourteenth — its blood, the next verse directs, to be struck on the doorposts.
7:
On Exodus 12:7 — “And they shall take of the blood, and strike it on the two side posts and on the upper door post of the houses, wherein they shall eat it” — the witnesses mark the blood upon the doorposts.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the act. Keil and Delitzsch: some of the blood put on the posts and lintel. Adam Clarke: done by dipping a bunch of hyssop in the blood and sprinkling it (Exodus 12:22); an emblem of the blood of Christ shed for sin. Ellicott: the destroying influence would enter by the door; the blood above and beside it the safeguard.

The verse directs the blood. Some of the lamb’s blood is struck on the doorposts and lintel. The blood marks the houses for sparing.

So the witnesses see the blood of the slain lamb applied to the doorposts as the appointed safeguard. The witnesses note the act: “they shall take of the blood, and strike it on the two side posts and on the upper door post” — some of the lamb’s blood, caught in a basin, was to be struck or sprinkled on the lintel and the two side-posts of each house; Clarke explains it was done by dipping a bunch of hyssop in the blood and sprinkling it (Exodus 12:22). They mark the place chosen: the door, by which (as Ellicott observes) the destroying influence would enter the house; the blood above and on either side of the door marked the entrance as protected, so that the destroyer would not come in. They observe the houses specified: “wherein they shall eat it” — the blood outside, the lamb within; the same households sheltered by the blood feast on the lamb. They note the typology the witnesses richly draw: the blood of the paschal lamb on the door is an emblem of the blood of Christ, shed for sin and applied to the believer; as the sprinkled blood sheltered Israel from the destroyer, so the blood of Christ shelters the soul from judgment. They observe that it was not enough that the lamb be slain; its blood must be applied, struck on the very dwelling, the personal appropriation of the sacrifice. They note that the blood, not the worth of the house or its people, was the safeguard. The verse thus directs the blood: struck on the doorposts of the sheltered houses — the lamb within to be eaten, as the next verse says, that night.
8:
On Exodus 12:8 — “And they shall eat the flesh in that night, roast with fire, and unleavened bread; and with bitter herbs they shall eat it” — the witnesses mark the eating of the lamb with its accompaniments.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the meal. Keil and Delitzsch trace the directions. Adam Clarke: roast, perhaps against the Egyptian custom of raw flesh; bitter herbs. Ellicott: roasting the simplest and only mode open to all, lacking cauldrons. The Reformed Gill notes the bitter herbs a memorial of the bitterness of bondage.

The verse orders the meal. The lamb is eaten that night, roast, with unleavened bread and bitter herbs. The feast recalls bondage and haste.

So the witnesses see the manner of the paschal meal prescribed: the roasted lamb eaten with unleavened bread and bitter herbs. The witnesses note the time and preparation: “in that night, roast with fire” — the lamb was to be eaten the same night it was slain, and roasted whole over the fire; Ellicott observes that roasting was the simplest and most primitive mode, and the only one open to all, since the common people had no cauldrons large enough to boil a whole lamb. They mark the unleavened bread: eaten with the lamb, the bread without leaven, signifying (as the chapter later shows) the haste of their departure and the purging out of the old leaven. They observe the bitter herbs: “with bitter herbs they shall eat it” — the expositors take these as a memorial of the bitterness of their bondage in Egypt, the harsh affliction from which they were being redeemed; the sweetness of the lamb eaten with the bitterness of their servitude remembered. They note the typology some draw: the bitter herbs answer to the repentance and sense of sin’s bitterness with which the believer feeds on Christ. They observe that the meal is no mere supper but a sacred feast, every element appointed: the lamb for redemption, the bitter herbs for the bondage, the unleavened bread for the haste and purity. They note that the eating, not only the slaying, belongs to the rite: the lamb is to be fed upon. The verse thus orders the meal: roast lamb, unleavened bread, bitter herbs — the roasting further specified in the next verse.
9:
On Exodus 12:9 — “Eat not of it raw, nor sodden at all with water, but roast with fire; his head with his legs, and with the purtenance thereof” — the witnesses mark the lamb roasted whole.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the manner. Adam Clarke: the entrails (purtenance) not abused for divination; the lamb roasted whole. Ellicott: roasted whole, “not a bone broken” (Exodus 12:46); Justin Martyr describes the two wooden spits forming a cross. Wesley: not raw, half-roasted, but thoroughly dressed.

The verse forbids other cooking. The lamb is neither raw nor boiled but wholly roast. The whole, unbroken lamb is presented.

So the witnesses see the manner of preparing the lamb strictly defined: roasted whole, neither raw nor boiled. The witnesses note the prohibitions: “Eat not of it raw, nor sodden at all with water” — the lamb was not to be eaten underdone or half-roasted (Wesley), nor boiled in water, but only roasted with fire; some of the expositors suggest these rules set the rite apart from heathen customs, whether the eating of raw flesh or the boiling practiced elsewhere. They mark the wholeness required: “his head with his legs, and with the purtenance thereof” — the lamb roasted entire, with head, legs, and inner parts, not cut up or dismembered. They observe Clarke’s note that the entrails were not to be removed and abused for divination, as the heathen did, but roasted within the whole. They observe the typology the witnesses draw, especially around the unbroken lamb: as not a bone of the paschal lamb was to be broken (Exodus 12:46), so not a bone of Christ was broken on the cross (John 19:36), the type fulfilled in the crucified Lamb of God. They note the ancient observation (Justin Martyr) that the lamb was spitted on two crossed pieces of wood, a transverse and an upright, forming the figure of a cross. They observe that the whole, unbroken, fire-roasted lamb both distinguished the rite and prefigured Christ. They note that the fire may also speak of the wrath endured. The verse thus orders the roasting whole: the unbroken lamb — of which, the next verse adds, nothing was to remain.
10:
On Exodus 12:10 — “And ye shall let nothing of it remain until the morning; and that which remaineth… ye shall burn with fire” — the witnesses mark that nothing of the lamb was to remain.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: to prevent putrefaction, for nothing offered to God should be subject to corruption; so the body of Christ saw no corruption. Ellicott: that there be neither profanation nor superstitious use of the remainder. The Reformed Gill draws the type: a whole Christ to be received and fed upon by faith.

The verse forbids any remainder. Nothing of the lamb is to be left; the rest is burned. The whole lamb is consumed or destroyed.

So the witnesses see the rite require that the lamb be wholly consumed, with nothing left over. The witnesses note the command: “ye shall let nothing of it remain until the morning” — the lamb was to be entirely eaten that night, and whatever could not be eaten “ye shall burn with fire,” destroyed rather than kept. They mark the reasons the expositors give. Clarke notes a practical reason joined to a sacred one: in so hot a land the flesh would soon putrefy, and it was not fitting that what had been offered to God should be subject to corruption; he draws the type that the body of Christ, offered to God, saw no corruption. Ellicott adds that nothing was to remain lest the leftover be profaned or put to superstitious use, the sacred thing degraded into a relic or charm. They observe the typology Gill presses: the eating of the whole lamb, leaving nothing, pictures the receiving of a whole Christ by faith — Christ in both his natures and all his offices, fed upon entirely, not in part. They note the completeness the rite demands: the lamb is given wholly to the household’s use that night, and what remains is given wholly to the fire; nothing is hoarded or carried over. They observe that this guards both against waste in corruption and against superstition. They note that the believer is to feed on the whole Christ, holding nothing back. The verse thus forbids any remainder: the whole lamb eaten or burned — eaten, as the next verse adds, in the posture of haste.
11:
On Exodus 12:11 — “And thus shall ye eat it; with your loins girded, your shoes on your feet, and your staff in your hand; and ye shall eat it in haste: it is the LORD’S passover” — the witnesses mark the posture of haste and the name of the rite.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the readiness. Adam Clarke: loins girded, the long garments tucked up for travel; shoes on, staff in hand — the posture of those about to journey. Ellicott: these injunctions referred to the impending departure, not repeated at later Passovers. Wesley: “the LORD’s passover” — a sign of his passing over them when he destroyed the Egyptians.

The verse fixes the posture. They eat girded, shod, staff in hand, in haste. The meal is the LORD’s passover, eaten as for a journey.

So the witnesses see the paschal meal eaten in the very posture of departure, and named the LORD’s passover. The witnesses note the attire of readiness: “with your loins girded, your shoes on your feet, and your staff in your hand” — the long loose garments tucked up in the girdle for travel, sandals on (not the bare feet of those at rest in the house), staff in hand as for a march; all the marks of pilgrims poised to set out. They mark the manner: “ye shall eat it in haste” — not a leisurely feast but a hurried meal, fitting men about to be summoned out of Egypt at any moment. They observe Ellicott’s note that these particular injunctions had reference to that impending departure and were not generally observed at later Passovers, when Israel ate at ease in their own land. They observe the great name given: “it is the LORD’s passover” — Wesley explains the word as a sign of God’s passing over the houses of Israel when he destroyed the Egyptians; the rite takes its name from the sparing mercy it commemorates. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: the pilgrim posture befits the redeemed, who are to hold themselves ready to leave the world, girded for their journey to the promised rest, feeding on the Lamb as those who do not abide here. They observe that haste and readiness mark the night of redemption. The verse thus fixes the posture and the name: the passover eaten in haste, as for a journey — the ground of which, God’s passing over, the next verses declare.
12:
On Exodus 12:12 — “For I will pass through the land of Egypt this night, and will smite all the firstborn… and against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgment: I am the LORD” — the witnesses mark the judgment on Egypt and its gods.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the judgment. Adam Clarke: the slaying of the firstborn of the sacred beasts was judgment on the gods of Egypt. Ellicott: “gods” far preferable to “princes.” The Reformed Gill: consistent with God’s omnipresence, the exertion of his power. Calvin: God declares himself judge against the false gods, which then appeared utterly unable to help.

The verse announces the stroke. God will pass through, smite the firstborn, and judge Egypt’s gods. The deliverance is a judgment on idolatry.

So the witnesses see God announce the dreadful stroke that is the ground of the Passover: the smiting of the firstborn and the judgment of Egypt’s gods. The witnesses note the divine action: “I will pass through the land of Egypt this night” — understood, Gill notes, consistently with God’s omnipresence, as the manifestation of his powerful presence and the exertion of his might in the coming event. They mark the blow: “and will smite all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, both man and beast” — the tenth and crowning plague, the death of every firstborn. They observe the further judgment: “and against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgment” — the expositors prefer “gods” to “princes,” and Clarke explains that, since various animals were held sacred by the Egyptians, the slaying of the firstborn of their beasts was itself a judgment on their gods; Calvin adds that God thus declared himself the judge against the false gods, whose utter impotence to help then plainly appeared. They note the self-attestation: “I am the LORD” — the seal of his sovereign authority and the assurance that he will surely do it. They observe that the deliverance of Israel is at once the judgment of Egypt and the overthrow of its idols; salvation and judgment go together. They note that the destroying stroke makes the sheltering blood all the more precious. The verse thus announces the judgment: the firstborn smitten, the gods judged — from which the blood, the next verse promises, shall shelter Israel.
13:
On Exodus 12:13 — “And the blood shall be to you for a token… and when I see the blood, I will pass over you, and the plague shall not be upon you to destroy you” — the witnesses mark the blood as the token of sparing.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the sign. Adam Clarke: the blood the sign to the destroying angel that the house is under God’s protection, none in it to be injured. Ellicott: rather, “a token for you” — a token to Me on your behalf. Keil and Delitzsch: when God sees the blood he will pass over. The Reformed Gill notes the blood the security of the Israelites.

The verse promises protection. Seeing the blood, God passes over and the plague spares. The blood is the ground of safety.

So the witnesses see the heart of the Passover: the blood that shelters Israel from the destroying stroke. The witnesses note the office of the blood: “the blood shall be to you for a token” — Ellicott observes the better sense is “a token for you,” that is, a token to God on the people’s behalf; Clarke takes it as the sign to the destroying angel that the house is under God’s protection and none in it to be harmed. They mark the great promise: “when I see the blood, I will pass over you” — God himself regards the sprinkled blood, and at the sight of it passes over the marked house, sparing it; the plague that smites Egypt does not fall on Israel. They observe the precise condition: it is the blood God looks upon, not the worthiness of the people; the Israelites within were sinners like the Egyptians, but the blood made the difference. They note the typology the witnesses press throughout: as God passed over the blood-marked houses, so the blood of Christ, the true Passover, shelters the believer from the judgment due to sin; what God sees and accepts is the blood of the Lamb. They observe that the safety lay wholly in the appointed token: the lamb slain and its blood applied, not in any merit of the sheltered. They note that the plague “shall not be upon you to destroy you” — the destroyer is barred by the blood. They observe that this verse is the gospel in figure: salvation through the blood of the Lamb. The verse thus promises protection: the blood seen, the house passed over — the night to be kept ever after, as the next verse commands, as a memorial.
14:
On Exodus 12:14 — “And this day shall be unto you for a memorial; and ye shall keep it a feast to the LORD… by an ordinance for ever” — the witnesses mark the perpetual memorial.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the lasting feast. Adam Clarke: a memorial of God’s justice and mercy, annually observed with solemn religious joy. Ellicott: the Passover continued in the Eucharist (1 Corinthians 5:7); the Easter celebration corresponds. The Reformed Gill: a memorable day, more so for the Messiah. Calvin: an “ordinance for ever” means perpetuity, yet such as Christ fulfils.

The verse makes the day a memorial. The Passover is to be kept as a perpetual feast. Redemption is to be remembered for all generations.

So the witnesses see God make the night of deliverance a perpetual memorial to be kept by Israel forever. The witnesses note the command: “this day shall be unto you for a memorial” — the day was to keep alive the remembrance of God’s great act, of his justice on Egypt and his mercy on Israel; Clarke marks it as a memorial of both the severity and the goodness of God. They mark the form of the remembering: “ye shall keep it a feast to the LORD… by an ordinance for ever” — an annual festival, celebrated with solemn religious joy throughout their generations. They observe Calvin’s note on “for ever”: the word denotes perpetuity, yet a perpetuity such as belongs to a type, finding its true and lasting fulfilment in Christ; the figure endures until the substance comes. They observe the Christian application the expositors draw, especially Ellicott: the Passover is continued and fulfilled in the Eucharist, “Christ our passover is sacrificed for us” (1 Corinthians 5:7), and the Church’s Easter answers in time to the paschal season. They note Gill’s sense that the day was made memorable by the Exodus and more memorable still by the death of the Messiah at this season. They observe that God appoints not only the act of redemption but its lasting commemoration, that the mercy be never forgotten. They note that a redeemed people must keep their redemption in remembrance. The verse thus makes the day a memorial: a perpetual feast to the LORD — the feast of unleavened bread now joined to it in the next verses.
15:
On Exodus 12:15 — “Seven days shall ye eat unleavened bread… whosoever eateth leavened bread… that soul shall be cut off from Israel” — the witnesses mark the feast of unleavened bread.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the distinct feast. Adam Clarke: a distinct ordinance; the Passover on the fourteenth, the feast of unleavened bread from the fifteenth, seven days. Keil and Delitzsch trace the law. The Reformed Gill: from the evening of the fourteenth to the twenty-first, a distinct festival. Calvin: the punishment enacted for mingling leaven with the Paschal feast.

The verse joins the unleavened bread. For seven days no leaven is eaten, on pain of being cut off. The feast of purity follows the Passover.

So the witnesses see the Passover joined to the seven-day feast of unleavened bread, with a solemn penalty for leaven. The witnesses note the institution: “seven days shall ye eat unleavened bread” — from the fifteenth to the twenty-first of the month, a week in which all leaven was put away and only unleavened bread eaten; the expositors note this was a distinct ordinance, closely joined to the Passover yet a feast in its own right. They mark the command to purge the leaven: “even the first day ye shall put away leaven out of your houses” — the leaven was to be removed from the very start, the houses cleansed of it. They observe the penalty: “whosoever eateth leavened bread… that soul shall be cut off from Israel” — to violate the ordinance by eating leaven during the feast was to be cut off from the people, so seriously was the command guarded. They note the typology the New Testament draws and the witnesses echo: leaven, which spreads and corrupts, is a figure of sin and of the “old leaven” of malice and wickedness, which the redeemed are to purge out, keeping the feast with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth (1 Corinthians 5:7–8). They observe that the feast of unleavened bread teaches the purity of life that befits a redeemed people; the deliverance is to issue in holiness. They note that the haste of the departure, which left no time to leaven the bread, is also commemorated. The verse thus joins the unleavened bread: seven days of purity — the first and seventh days, the next verse adds, holy convocations.
16:
On Exodus 12:16 — “And in the first day there shall be an holy convocation, and in the seventh day… no manner of work shall be done in them, save that which every man must eat” — the witnesses mark the holy convocations.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the assemblies. Adam Clarke: the first mention of an assembly gathered purely for religious worship, called holy convocations. Keil and Delitzsch trace it. Ellicott: the first and last of the seven days kept as sabbaths, save the preparing of food. Wesley: a solemn day for the people to assemble.

The verse appoints the sacred assemblies. The first and seventh days are holy convocations with rest from work. The feast is framed by worship.

So the witnesses see the seven days of unleavened bread framed at beginning and end by sacred assemblies of rest and worship. The witnesses note the institution: “in the first day there shall be an holy convocation, and in the seventh day… an holy convocation” — the opening and closing days of the feast were to be kept as holy days, the people gathered for worship. They mark Clarke’s observation of the significance of the term: this is the first place in Scripture where we read of an assembly collected purely for the purpose of religious worship, called a “holy convocation”; the redeemed people are to come together to worship the God who saved them. They observe the rest commanded: “no manner of work shall be done in them, save that which every man must eat” — these days were to be kept as sabbaths, free from ordinary labour, with the one exception of preparing necessary food; Ellicott notes the easing of the rest to allow the dressing of the festal meal. They note that the convocations sanctify the feast: it is not only a domestic remembrance but a gathered, public act of worship, opening and closing in solemn assembly. They observe that God appoints both the rest and the worship, that the people’s deliverance be marked by holy joy and the laying aside of common toil. They note that the rhythm of work ceased and worship rendered befits a people called to be holy. The verse thus appoints the holy convocations: the feast framed by sacred assembly and rest — the keeping of which the next verse grounds in the day of the Exodus.
17:
On Exodus 12:17 — “And ye shall observe the feast of unleavened bread; for in this selfsame day have I brought your armies out of the land of Egypt” — the witnesses mark the ground of the feast in the Exodus.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the reason. Keil and Delitzsch: observe the Mazzoth, for on this day God brought their armies out. Adam Clarke: “selfsame day” — in the body or strength of the day; probably they began their march about daybreak. Ellicott notes the word “armies” applied to Israel (Exodus 6:26). The Reformed Gill: a distinct feast from the passover, kept seven days.

The verse grounds the feast in the deliverance. The unleavened bread is kept because on that day God brought Israel out. The rite memorializes the Exodus.

So the witnesses see the feast of unleavened bread grounded in the very day of the deliverance. The witnesses note the command repeated: “ye shall observe the feast of unleavened bread” — the keeping of the seven days is enjoined again, that it be diligently marked. They mark the reason given: “for in this selfsame day have I brought your armies out of the land of Egypt” — the feast is to be kept because on this very day God led Israel out; the observance is tied to the event it commemorates. They observe the term “armies” (or “hosts”) applied to Israel, which Ellicott connects with the earlier description (Exodus 6:26): the people went out not as a disorganized rabble but as marshalled hosts, an army under God’s leading. They note Clarke’s observation on “the selfsame day,” literally “in the body or strength of the day,” suggesting they began their march about daybreak (though Deuteronomy says by night), in the full vigour of the day of deliverance. They observe that the feast is thus rooted in history: a real, dated act of God, not a mere seasonal custom; Israel keeps the bread of haste because God brought them out in haste on this day. They note that the perpetual observance binds each generation to the founding deliverance. They observe that the rite preaches the Exodus to all who keep it. The verse thus grounds the feast: kept because God brought the armies out on this day — the time precisely fixed in the next verse.
18:
On Exodus 12:18 — “In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month at even, ye shall eat unleavened bread, until the one and twentieth day… at even” — the witnesses mark the exact bounds of the feast.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the dating. Charles Ellicott: the evening intended is that with which the fourteenth closed, the end of the fourteenth and beginning of the fifteenth; the bounds precisely set. The Reformed Gill: the first month, now Abib or Nisan.

The verse fixes the dates. The unleavened bread runs from the evening of the fourteenth to that of the twenty-first. The feast has exact, appointed bounds.

So the witnesses see the precise temporal bounds of the feast laid down. The witnesses note the careful dating: “in the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month at even… until the one and twentieth day of the month at even” — the eating of unleavened bread is fixed to run from one evening to another, seven days exactly. They mark Ellicott’s clarification of which evening is meant: the evening intended at the start is not that with which the fourteenth began, but the one with which it closed — the end of the fourteenth and the beginning of the fifteenth; so the seven days of unleavened bread run from the evening following the Passover to the evening of the twenty-first. They observe the exactness with which God appoints the season: the feast is not left to convenience but fixed to a definite span, by the calendar God himself had reset (v. 2). They note Gill’s reminder that this “first month” is the month Abib (Nisan), now made the head of the year. They observe that the precision guards the ordinance from confusion and ensures all Israel keeps the feast together, at the same time, in every generation; a common calendar binds the people in a common observance. They note that God is exact in the times of his worship. They observe that the bounds, evening to evening, follow the Hebrew reckoning of the day. The verse thus fixes the dates: the fourteenth at even to the twenty-first at even — the prohibition of leaven within them pressed again in the next verse.
19:
On Exodus 12:19 — “Seven days shall there be no leaven found in your houses: for whosoever eateth that which is leavened… shall be cut off… whether he be a stranger, or born in the land” — the witnesses mark the strict purging of leaven.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rigour. Adam Clarke: the Jews instituted the most rigorous search through every part of their houses, removing all leaven. Ellicott: a “stranger” means a foreigner adopted into the nation, circumcised, a full proselyte. Wesley: a stranger is a proselyte; heathens were not concerned in the passover.

The verse presses the purge. No leaven is to be found for seven days, native or stranger alike, on pain of being cut off. The cleansing is searching and universal.

So the witnesses see the command against leaven pressed with great rigour and extended to all within Israel. The witnesses note the thoroughness demanded: “seven days shall there be no leaven found in your houses” — not merely that none be eaten, but that none be even found; Clarke records how the later Jews, to meet this command to the letter, instituted a most rigorous search through every part of their houses on the eve of the feast, removing and destroying all leaven. They mark the penalty repeated: “whosoever eateth that which is leavened… shall be cut off” — the same solemn excision from the congregation for the breach. They observe the breadth of the law: “whether he be a stranger, or born in the land” — the “stranger,” the expositors explain, is not the heathen but the foreigner adopted into the nation, circumcised, a full proselyte; such, having joined Israel, were bound by the same ordinance as the native-born, while unconverted heathens had no part in the Passover at all. They note the typology the witnesses draw, continuing from verse 15: the searching out and destroying of every particle of leaven figures the believer’s diligent purging of all sin, the old leaven of corruption, from the heart and life. They observe that the law makes no exception for native or stranger: all who belong to the redeemed people must keep the feast in purity. They note the searching, universal character of the cleansing. The verse thus presses the purge: no leaven found, on pain of excision, for native and proselyte alike — the prohibition restated in the next verse.
20:
On Exodus 12:20 — “Ye shall eat nothing leavened; in all your habitations shall ye eat unleavened bread” — the witnesses mark the summary prohibition of leaven.

The Reformed Gill notes the comprehensiveness: bread, or anything else that had any leaven in it, was forbidden; in all their dwellings unleavened bread alone was to be eaten through the feast.

The verse repeats the rule plainly. Nothing leavened is to be eaten anywhere; only unleavened bread. The command is made total and universal.

So the witnesses see the law against leaven gathered into a plain, comprehensive summary. The witnesses note the totality of the prohibition: “ye shall eat nothing leavened” — Gill draws out the breadth, that not only leavened bread but anything whatever containing leaven was forbidden during the seven days; the rule admits no exception of kind. They mark the universality of place: “in all your habitations shall ye eat unleavened bread” — wherever an Israelite dwelt, in every house and dwelling-place, only unleavened bread was to be eaten; the command reached the whole people in all their homes, that none might keep the feast otherwise. They observe that this verse restates and seals the law already given, leaving no room for evasion: nothing leavened, anywhere, by anyone of the congregation. They note the emphasis the repetition lays on the matter: God presses the purging of leaven again and again, that its importance be felt; the feast of unleavened bread is no light or optional thing. They observe the continued typology: the total exclusion of leaven from every dwelling answers to the believer’s call to be wholly cleansed from sin, in every part of life; not one corner is to harbour the old leaven. They note that the command is made absolute and universal, binding the whole redeemed community. They observe that purity is to be entire, not partial. The verse thus repeats the rule: nothing leavened in any dwelling — before Moses, in the next verses, conveys the Passover command to the elders.

So the witnesses see the law against leaven gathered into a plain, comprehensive summary. They note the totality of the prohibition: “ye shall eat nothing leavened” — Gill draws out the breadth, that not only leavened bread but anything whatever containing leaven was forbidden during the seven days; the rule admits no exception of kind. They mark the universality of place: “in all your habitations shall ye eat unleavened bread” — wherever an Israelite dwelt, in every house and dwelling-place, only unleavened bread was to be eaten. They observe that this verse restates and seals the law already given, leaving no room for evasion: nothing leavened, anywhere, by anyone of the congregation. They note the emphasis the repetition lays: God presses the purging of leaven again and again, that its importance be felt. They observe the continued typology: the total exclusion of leaven from every dwelling answers to the believer’s call to be wholly cleansed from sin, in every part of life, not one corner harbouring the old leaven; purity is to be entire, not partial.
21:
On Exodus 12:21 — “Then Moses called for all the elders of Israel, and said unto them, Draw out and take you a lamb… and kill the passover” — the witnesses mark Moses conveying the command to the elders.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the agency. Adam Clarke: “kill the passover” — the lamb called by the name of the institution itself. Ellicott: directed to speak to all the congregation, he did so through the elders. Keil and Delitzsch: “draw out” (mashak), intransitive, withdraw. The Puritan Henry: Moses, a faithful steward, teaches Israel all God commanded. The Reformed Gill: the elders, heads of families and principal men.

The verse conveys the command. Moses calls the elders and bids them take and kill the passover lamb. The ordinance is delivered to the people through their heads.

So the witnesses see Moses faithfully convey the Passover command to the people through their elders. The witnesses note the agency: “Moses called for all the elders of Israel” — having been directed to speak to all the congregation (v. 3), Moses does so, as Ellicott notes, mediately, through the elders, the heads of families and principal men, who would carry the instruction to the rest; Gill identifies them as elders not merely in age but in office. They mark Henry’s observation of Moses’ fidelity: as a faithful steward in God’s house, he teaches the children of Israel to observe all that God commanded, delivering the instructions as fully as he received them. They observe the charge: “Draw out and take you a lamb… and kill the passover” — the expositors note the phrase “draw out” (or, some read, “withdraw,” the verb being intransitive) as bidding them go and fetch a lamb from the flock for each household; and they mark Clarke’s observation that the lamb is here called “the passover” itself, the sacrifice named by the name of the institution it served. They note that the actual keeping of the rite now begins: from the divine command Moses turns to its execution among the people. They observe that the orderly conveyance, from God to Moses to the elders to the families, ensures all Israel keeps the feast. They note the dignity given the lamb in being called the Passover. The verse thus conveys the command: take and kill the passover — with the blood, the next verse directs, to be applied by hyssop.
22:
On Exodus 12:22 — “And ye shall take a bunch of hyssop, and dip it in the blood… and strike the lintel and the two side posts… and none of you shall go out at the door of his house until the morning” — the witnesses mark the applying of the blood and the staying within.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the means. Keil and Delitzsch and Clarke: the hyssop (ezob), a bunch, dipped in the blood in the basin. Ellicott: probably the caper plant, growing in the Sinai region, fit for sprinkling. Wesley: none to go out till morning, when they would be called to march out; they went out very early.

The verse details the act. The blood is applied with hyssop, and none may leave the house till morning. Within the blood-marked door is safety.

So the witnesses see the practical applying of the blood and the charge to remain within the sheltered house. The witnesses note the instrument: “a bunch of hyssop” — a small plant (the expositors debating its precise identity, Ellicott favouring the caper that grows plentifully in the Sinai region), tied in a bunch and dipped in the blood that was in the basin, then used to strike or sprinkle the blood on the lintel and the two side-posts. They mark that the hyssop, often associated in Scripture with cleansing and purification, is here the means of applying the atoning blood. They observe the strict charge to stay indoors: “none of you shall go out at the door of his house until the morning” — the Israelites were to keep within their blood-marked houses through the night; Calvin notes the reason, lest they mix with the Egyptians and so be exposed to the destruction, and that they might learn they too were liable to judgment but for the sheltering blood. They observe Wesley’s note that they were to stay until the morning, when they would be summoned to march out of Egypt. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: safety lay within the blood, and to step outside its protection was to court the destroyer; the redeemed must abide under the shelter of the sacrifice. They observe that the blood applied and the household kept within together secured them. They note that the hyssop and the basin are the appointed means, not to be neglected. The verse thus details the act: blood applied by hyssop, the household kept within — the ground of which, God’s passing over, the next verse declares.
23:
On Exodus 12:23 — “For the LORD will pass through to smite the Egyptians; and when he seeth the blood… the LORD will pass over the door, and will not suffer the destroyer to come in… to smite you” — the witnesses mark the LORD’s passing over and the barring of the destroyer.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the “plague” here called “the destroyer” (Hebrews 11:28); God employed an angel as his agent. Wesley: the destroying angel — whether a good or an evil angel, we have not light to determine. The Reformed Calvin: by staying within, kept under the protection of the blood.

The verse gives the ground of safety. Seeing the blood, the LORD passes over and bars the destroyer. The blood turns the stroke aside.

So the witnesses see the reason for the blood and the staying-within: the LORD’s passing over the blood-marked door. The witnesses note the coming judgment: “the LORD will pass through to smite the Egyptians” — God goes through the land to strike the firstborn. They mark the saving sight: “when he seeth the blood upon the lintel, and on the two side posts, the LORD will pass over the door” — at the sight of the sprinkled blood, God spares the marked house, passing over it; the blood, seen and accepted, is the ground of the sparing. They observe the agency of the stroke: “will not suffer the destroyer to come in” — the plague is here personified as “the destroyer,” which the New Testament also names (Hebrews 11:28); the expositors take this as an angel employed by God as his agent in the slaying. They note the question some raise about this destroyer: Wesley owns that whether it was a good or an evil angel, we have not light to determine; God’s instrument either way. They observe the comfort the verse gives: the blood not only marks the house but actively bars the destroyer, so that he cannot enter where the blood is found; the safety is sure. They note the typology fulfilled: the blood of Christ, seen by God, turns away the judgment due to the believer, the destroyer barred from the soul sheltered by the Lamb. They observe that God himself, not the people, keeps the door. The verse thus gives the ground: the blood seen, the destroyer barred — the rite to be kept ever after, as the next verses charge.
24:
On Exodus 12:24 — “And ye shall observe this thing for an ordinance to thee and to thy sons for ever” — the witnesses mark the perpetual obligation.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “this thing” not the sprinkling of the blood, never repeated after the first occasion, but the sacrifice of the lamb. The Reformed Gill: not the blood-sprinkling, peculiar to the Egyptian passover, but the whole feast of the passover. Calvin: he repeats the precept of its annual celebration.

The verse binds future generations. The rite is to be observed for ever by them and their sons. The Passover is a lasting ordinance.

So the witnesses see the Passover bound upon Israel as a perpetual ordinance for all generations. The witnesses note the command: “ye shall observe this thing for an ordinance to thee and to thy sons for ever” — the keeping of the feast is laid not only on the present generation but on their children after them, throughout their history. They mark the careful distinction the expositors draw as to what “this thing” means: not, as Ellicott and Gill note, the striking of the blood on the doorposts, which was peculiar to this first Passover in Egypt and never repeated afterward, but the sacrifice and feast of the lamb, the substance of the ordinance, which was to continue. They observe that the saving application of the blood belonged to the unique night of deliverance, while the annual commemoration — the slaying and eating of the lamb — was to be perpetual. They note Calvin’s point that here the precept of the annual celebration is repeated, that the memory of the deliverance might never perish. They observe that God binds the rite on the sons as on the fathers, that each generation in turn might own the redemption as its own and keep it alive; the obligation passes down the line of the covenant people. They note that the perpetuity, as before, is that of a type, until fulfilled in Christ. They observe that the children’s observance is expressly required. The verse thus binds the future: the ordinance kept by them and their sons for ever — especially, the next verse adds, when they come to the land.
25:
On Exodus 12:25 — “And it shall come to pass, when ye be come to the land which the LORD will give you, according as he hath promised, that ye shall keep this service” — the witnesses mark the keeping of the rite in the promised land.

The Reformed Gill: the land of Canaan, toward which they were about to set forward, and in a few years would possess. Calvin: when they have come into the land, the recollection of their deliverance is yearly to be revived by this rite.

The verse looks to the land. When settled in Canaan, they are still to keep the service. The rite reaches from Egypt into the inheritance.

So the witnesses see the Passover commanded not only for the night of deliverance but for all their settled life in the promised land. The witnesses note the forward look: “when ye be come to the land which the LORD will give you, according as he hath promised” — God speaks of Canaan as already given by promise, the land toward which Israel was now to set out and which, Gill notes, they would possess in a few years; the keeping of the feast is enjoined for the time of their settled inheritance. They mark the continuity this establishes: the rite born in Egypt, on the eve of the Exodus, was to be carried forward and kept in the land of rest; the redeemed were to commemorate their redemption not only in the moment of escape but ever after, in their homes in Canaan. They observe Calvin’s point that, once come into the land, the recollection of their deliverance was to be yearly revived by this service, lest the memory of so great a grace should fade with time and prosperity. They note that the command guards against forgetfulness: the very generations that would enjoy the fruit of the deliverance, secure in the land, were the ones most apt to forget its cost, and so were bound to keep the feast. They observe that God ties the gift of the land to the remembrance of the redemption that led to it. They note that the service spans the whole pilgrimage, from bondage to inheritance. The verse thus looks to the land: the service kept when they are come to Canaan — the occasion, the next verse adds, for instructing the children.
26:
On Exodus 12:26 — “And it shall come to pass, when your children shall say unto you, What mean ye by this service?” — the witnesses mark the children’s question and the rite’s teaching.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the annual service was a wise provision to keep the wonderful deliverance in remembrance; from remotest antiquity feasts and rites have been used to preserve the memory of great events. The Reformed Gill: when children ask of the killing and eating of the lamb, the bitter herbs, the unleavened bread.

The verse anticipates the children. They will ask the meaning of the service, prompting instruction. The rite teaches the next generation.

So the witnesses see the Passover designed to provoke the children’s questions and so to teach each new generation the redemption. The witnesses note the foreseen inquiry: “when your children shall say unto you, What mean ye by this service?” — the strange and solemn rite — the killing and roasting of the lamb, the bitter herbs, the unleavened bread, the whole unusual observance — would naturally move the children to ask its meaning; Gill lists the very elements that would prompt the question. They mark the wisdom of this, which Clarke draws out: the annual keeping of the feast was a most wise provision to preserve the memory of the wonderful deliverance; from the remotest antiquity the institution of feasts and rites has served to keep alive the remembrance of great events, and here God ordains a rite that, by its very strangeness, would prompt the young to inquire and the old to instruct. They observe that the children’s question is not a difficulty but an opportunity: it opens the door for the parents to recount the Exodus, the night of the passing-over, the redemption by blood. They note that the feast is thus a standing catechism, teaching the covenant story from generation to generation through the asking and answering. They observe that God provides for the handing-down of the faith within the family. They note that the rite preaches by raising the question it then answers. The verse thus anticipates the children: their question the occasion of teaching — the answer of the Passover’s meaning to be given them, as the chapter continues.
27:
"That ye shall say, It is the sacrifice of the LORD's passover, who passed over the houses of the children of Israel in Egypt, when he smote the Egyptians, and delivered our houses. And the people bowed the head and worshipped." The witnesses note the explanation of the Passover and the people's worship. On "It is the sacrifice of the LORD's passover, who passed over the houses of the children of Israel," the grammatical voices note the meaning. Ellicott and Gill note that "ye shall say, It is the sacrifice of the LORD's passover, who passed over the houses of the children of Israel in Egypt, when he smote the Egyptians, and delivered our houses" — the answer to the children's question (v.26): the Passover is the sacrifice commemorating the LORD's passing over the houses of Israel (marked with the blood) when he smote the Egyptians' firstborn; the meaning of the feast (the deliverance by the blood); the catechesis (the redemption explained to the children).

On "who passed over the houses... and delivered our houses," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the deliverance. Calvin and Wesley note the LORD's "passing over" (sparing the blood-marked houses while smiting Egypt) — the deliverance by the blood of the lamb (a type of Christ, the blood that shelters from the destroyer, 1 Corinthians 5:7; Hebrews 11:28); the redemption commemorated.

On "the people bowed the head and worshipped," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the worship: "the people bowed the head and worshipped" — the people's reverent response (bowing in worship and thanksgiving for the promised deliverance); the faith that received the word and worshipped (Hebrews 11:28, "through faith he kept the passover"); the gratitude and submission of the redeemed. The verse displays the explanation of the Passover and the people's worship.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the meaning — "It is the sacrifice of the LORD's passover... and delivered our houses," the deliverance by the blood (v.26, Ellicott, Gill); "who passed over the houses... and delivered our houses," the redemption (1 Corinthians 5:7, Calvin, Wesley); "the people bowed the head and worshipped," the worship (Hebrews 11:28, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the worshipping people. The verse marks the passover: the LORD passed over. The verse displays the blood: the deliverance. The verse displays the worship: bowed the head. The verse displays the faith: receiving it. The explanation displays the deliverance by the blood. The verse displays the people: worshipping. The verse displays the LORD: passing over. The verse displays the redemption: commemorated. The explanation of the Passover and the people's worship display the deliverance by the blood: the LORD passed over and delivered our houses; the people bowed and worshipped (1 Corinthians 5:7; Hebrews 11:28) — the redemption commemorated, the faith that received it and worshipped. The verse displays the keeping of the command (v.28).
28:
"And the children of Israel went away, and did as the LORD had commanded Moses and Aaron, so did they." The very brief verse notes Israel's obedience to the Passover command. The grammatical voices note the obedience. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the children of Israel went away, and did as the LORD had commanded Moses and Aaron, so did they" — the people obeyed, doing all as the LORD had commanded (keeping the Passover, vv.1-27: taking the lamb, striking the blood, eating the flesh); the prompt obedience of faith; the keeping of the ordinance; the faith that obeyed (Hebrews 11:28).

On "did as the LORD had commanded," the witnesses note the faith. Gill notes the obedience (doing all as commanded) — the faith that kept the Passover; the prompt compliance; the people's obedience.

On "so did they," the grammatical-historical note the completeness: "so did they" — the full obedience (exactly as commanded); the keeping of the ordinance; the faith of the redeemed (the blood applied, the deliverance secured, vv.13); the providence (the obedience that brought the deliverance). The verse displays Israel's obedience to the Passover command.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "the children of Israel went away, and did as the LORD had commanded... so did they," the keeping (vv.1-27, Keil, Gill); "did as the LORD had commanded," the faith (Hebrews 11:28, Gill); "so did they," the completeness (v.13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the obedient people. The verse marks the obedience: as commanded. The verse displays the Passover: kept. The verse displays the faith: obeying. The verse displays the deliverance: secured. The obedience displays Israel keeping the Passover by faith. The verse displays the people: obeying. The verse displays the command: kept. The verse displays the blood: applied. Israel's obedience to the Passover command displays the faith that kept it: they did as the LORD commanded (Hebrews 11:28) — the prompt obedience of faith, the blood applied, the deliverance secured. The verse displays the death of the firstborn (v.29).
29:
"And it came to pass, that at midnight the LORD smote all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh that sat on his throne unto the firstborn of the captive that was in the dungeon; and all the firstborn of cattle." The witnesses note the death of the firstborn at midnight — the crux of the last plague executed. On "at midnight the LORD smote all the firstborn in the land of Egypt," interpreters expound the judgment. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the great stroke — "at midnight the LORD smote all the firstborn in the land of Egypt": at the appointed hour (Exodus 11:4), the LORD (by the destroying angel, v.23; Hebrews 11:28) smote every firstborn in Egypt; the tenth and climactic plague executed (the judgment foretold, Exodus 11:5); the immediate hand of God (the LORD himself, the destroyer passing through Egypt); the awful midnight stroke (the dead of night, the land asleep, the sudden universal death).

On "from the firstborn of Pharaoh that sat on his throne unto the firstborn of the captive that was in the dungeon," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the universality. Wesley and Ellicott note the sweep of the judgment — from the highest (Pharaoh's firstborn, the heir of the throne) to the lowest (the captive in the dungeon, the meanest slave); none exempt (the leveling judgment of God, no rank or station spared, Exodus 11:5); the universal stroke (every house in Egypt with its dead, v.30).

On "and all the firstborn of cattle," the puritan and grammatical-historical note the gods judged: "and all the firstborn of cattle" — even the firstborn of the beasts smitten (the judgment on the cattle the Egyptians worshipped, Exodus 12:12, "against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgment"); the complete judgment (the recompense for Pharaoh's slaying of Israel's sons, Exodus 1:22; the doom for refusing God's firstborn, Exodus 4:22-23); the providence (the last plague that broke Pharaoh, freed Israel). The verse displays the death of the firstborn at midnight.

The witnesses agree the substance: at midnight the LORD smote every firstborn, from Pharaoh's to the captive's, and of the cattle (Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the last plague executed: "the LORD smote all the firstborn" — the tenth and climactic judgment, the LORD himself (by the destroyer, v.23; Hebrews 11:28) smiting at the appointed hour; the universal stroke from highest to lowest, none exempt; the judgment on Egypt's gods (the firstborn of the cattle, Exodus 12:12), the recompense for the slaying of Israel's sons (Exodus 1:22), the doom for refusing God's firstborn (Exodus 4:22-23). The verse displays the firstborn-death crux. The verse marks the hour: midnight. The verse displays the smiting: all the firstborn. The verse displays the sweep: throne to dungeon. The verse displays the judgment: climactic. The death displays the LORD's climactic judgment on Egypt. The verse displays the LORD: smiting. The verse displays the firstborn: slain. The verse displays the Egypt: judged. The death of the firstborn at midnight displays the crux: at midnight the LORD smote all the firstborn, from Pharaoh's to the captive's (Exodus 11:4-5; 12:12, 23; 1:22; 4:22-23) — the climactic judgment by the LORD's own hand, the universal stroke, the recompense and the doom. The verse displays the great cry (v.30).
30:
"And Pharaoh rose up in the night, he, and all his servants, and all the Egyptians; and there was a great cry in Egypt; for there was not a house where there was not one dead."
